sourceMovie.title: The Illusionist
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: The Illusionist (2006) stuck because it commits to its fantasy premise without apologizing—viewers remember set pieces and momentum as much as plot. Neil Burger's control of tone keeps the ride coherent. The fantasy and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Cinderella
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Illusionist clicked for you, Cinderella (2015) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and drama, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Maleficent
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If The Illusionist clicked for you, Maleficent (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in fantasy and romance, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after The Illusionist?
faq.answer: Try Cinderella, Maleficent, Beauty and the Beast—each shares DNA with The Illusionist while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is The Illusionist?
faq.answer: The Illusionist (2006) is best described as Fantasy, Drama, Thriller, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is The Illusionist worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this fantasy lane.
